catalyst.llm generation: Au-modified reduced graphene oxide coupled with carbon nitride (AurGOg-C 3 N 4 )
catalyst.source: Herein, we have fabricated Au-modified reduced graphene oxide coupled with carbon nitride (AurGOg-C 3 N 4 ) as novel 0D2D2D photocatalytic nanocomposites.
catalyst.context: Photocatalysis is regarded as one of promising technology for future clean and sustaniable energy applications.Herein, we have fabricated Au-modified reduced graphene oxide coupled with carbon nitride (AurGOg-C 3 N 4 ) as novel 0D2D2D photocatalytic nanocomposites.The optimized sample 2Au0.6rGOg-C 3 N 4 exhibits exceptional visible-light activity for water splititng and CO 2 reduction with quantum efficiency of 3.82 and 1.98 , respectively.Electrochemistry and ultraviolent photoemission are combined to determine the band alignments and elaborate associated water splitting path-way mechanism.It is validated that due to intrinsic deep valence band position of g-C 3 N 4 , the obtained nanocomposites exhibit unusual two-step two-electron way of water oxidization through intermediate H 2 O 2 catalyzed by rGO addition.The exceptional photoactivities are attributed to dual functions of enhanced charge separation and two-electron water oxidization facilitated by rGO and surface plasmon effect of decorated Au.Our work provides illuminations for low cost and high efficiency photocatalytic water splitting and CO 2 reduction applications.

catalyst.source: To fabricate rGOg-C 3 N 4 nanocomposites, 2 g of the fabricated g-C 3 N 4 was dispersed into a mixture of de-ionized H 2 O and ethanol (1:4).
catalyst.context: All the reagents were directly used as received without further purification. To prepare g-C 3 N 4 , approximately 10 g of urea was directly annealed in a muffle furnace at 550 C (0.5 C min -1 ) for 4 h, and then crushed into fine powder. To fabricate rGOg-C 3 N 4 nanocomposites, 2 g of the fabricated g-C 3 N 4 was dispersed into a mixture of de-ionized H 2 O and ethanol (1:4).The suspensions were kept under stirring for 2 h, and then 0.2, 0.4 and 0.6 g (10 wt , 20 wt and 30 wt ) of rGO was added to each suspension and vigorously stirred for 2 h.The suspensions were dried in at 85 C and then annealed at 450 C for 2 h to obtain different proportion rGOg-C 3 N 4 nanocomposites. Different wt.percentage (i.e.0.5 , 1.0 , 2.0 , and 4.0 ) of Au was photo-deposited on the photocatalysts surfaces.The required amount of Au solution (HAuCl 4 4H 2 O) was mixed with 50 mL de-ionized H 2 O.After that, 1 g of the rGOg-C 3 N 4 composites was dispersed in 5050 (100) mL watermethanol contained in a 250 mL beaker.Then, the appropriate amount of Au solution was added to each composite mixture and irradiated under visible-light irradiation for 2 h with continuous stirring.The samples were then centrifuged and dried at 85 C.The obtained slightly dark photocatalysts were labeled, according to the composition, as 0.5Au 0.6rGOg-C 3 N 4 , 1Au 0.6rGOg-C 3 N 4 , 2Au 0.6rGOg-C 3 N 4 and 4Au0.6rGOg-C 3 N 4 , respectively. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of the photocatalysts were obtained with Bruker D8 (Germany), using Cu K radiation source.The TEM images were captured with a JEOL-JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope.The SEM images were captured with Hitachi-S-4800 Scanning electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan) 67,68.The FT-IR spectra were taken with a Bruker Equinox-55 spectrophotometer.The XPS analysis was carried out with a Kratos-Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectroscope with Al (mono) X-ray source.The adventitious carbon (binding energy 284.6 eV) was used as a reference for samples calibration.The DRS spectra were obtained with UV-2550 (Shimadzu, Japan) UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectrophotometer.The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained at 420 nm excitation wavelength, using a PE-LS-55 spectro-fluoro-photometer 697071. The H 2 production experiments were performed in an online quartz cylindrical cell with area 6.5 cm 2 and volume 250 mL, linked to a closed gas circulatory system, where the evolved gases are detected with an online gas chromatograph TCD (Tech, GC-7900, N 2 gas carrier).For photocatalytic experiment, about 0.1 g of photocatalyst was dispersed in a mixture of 80 mL de-ionized H 2 O and 20 mL methanol (Note: in overall H 2 O splitting 100 mL pure H 2 O was used).The reaction system was evacuated for 30 min and then irradiated under visible-light ( 420 nm) with a 300 W Xe-lamp.The amount of H 2 gas was measured at regular time interval of 1 h. In the photocatalytic CO 2 reduction process, for each experiment, 0.1 g of a sample was dispersed in 50 mL water in a cylindrical steel reactor with 100 mL volume and glass surface area 6 cm 2 .A 300 W xenon arc lamp was used as the light source with a cut off filter of 420 nm.Highly pure CO 2 gas was entered into the reaction setup for reaching ambient pressure.The used photocatalyst was allowed to equilibrate in the CO 2 H 2 O system for 1 h.During irradiation, about 0.5 mL of gas products were continually taken from the reaction cell at a given time interval for subsequent CH 4 , CO, H 2 and O 2 concentration analysis by using a gas chromatograph (TCD and FID both). To measure the OH amount, about 0.05 g of photocatalyst was dispersed in a quartz reactor containing 40 mL of 0.001 M coumarin aqueous solution.The reactor was stirred for 30 min to attain the adsorption equilibrium.After that, the photocatalyst was irradiated under visible-light for 1 h and then centrifuged.A certain amount was taken into a Pyrex glass cell for the fluorescence measurement of 7-hydroxycoumarin at excitation wavelength of 420 nm, through a spectrofluorometer (Perkin-Elmer LS 55). To confirm the presence of H 2 O 2 as a reaction intermediate, 0.05 g sample was mixed with 40 mL deionized water by magnetic stirrer for 10 min in the absence of light, in order to keep the reactive system uniform adsorption equilibrium.After that the sample is exposed under light irradiation for 1 h and then centrifuged.The H 2 O 2 measurements test were performed in 2.0 mL reaction solution after the irradiation centrifuged with 0.5 mL of 1 o-tolidine in 0.1 M HCl.After 2 min, 1 M HCl (2 mL) was added to the above solution and the absorption spectrum of the solution was immediately recorded. For PEC studies, approximately 0.05 g of photocatalyst was dispersed in 2 mL isopropyl alcohol and stirred for 1 h.Then, 0.025 g of Macrogol-6000 was grinded into fine powder and added to the suspension.The suspension was kept in ultrasonic bath for 30 min, and then stirred for 1 h.After that, 0.05 mL of acetyl acetone was drop wise added to the suspension and stirred for 72 h.The cleaned conductivefluorine-doped tin-oxide (FTO) coated glasses were used as substrates for the preparation of photocatalysts films.The films were prepared by a doctor blade method-using Scotch tape as a spacer.The prepared photocatalysts films were dried in air for 30 min.The FTO glasses with films were cut into 1.0 cm 3.0 cm pieces.The films surface area was 1.0 cm 1.0 cm.For photoelectrode preparation, an electrical connection was made with FTO substrate using silver conducting paste linked to a copper-wire and enclosed in a glass tube.The working arithmetic surface area of the thin film was kept as 0.5 cm 0.5 cm and the extra area was covered with epoxy resin 53,54.The cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electron transfer number (n) experiments were performed in a three electrode configuration cell connected to a computer controlled CHI 920C work-station.The Pt and AgAgCl electrodes were used as the counter-electrode and the reference electrode, respectively.A glassy carbon (GC) electrode was clearly washed with Al 2 O 3 .After that, 7 L of the 0.5 wt Nafion solution was dropped onto the GC electrode surface.Then, 20 L of the catalyst solution having 1.5 mg mL -1 pasted on the surface of GC electrode and dried in oven at 40C.Before the test, the working electrode was immersed in the electrolyte solution and activated for 30 min to keep it stable.The CV curves were carried out in 0.1 M BMIMPF 6 solution (de-ionized water) in the presence of N 2 -environment with a 50 mVs scan rate.The Xelamp with 420 nm cut-off filter was used as the irradiation source.The electron-transfer number was recorded with a rotating disk-ring electrode (RRDE).The RRDE collection tests for 2Au0.6rGOCNwere carried out in N 2 -saturated ultrapure water a scan rate of 100 mVs and rotation speed of 1600 rpm.The disk potential was set at open circuit potential.The ring-potential was set at 0.9 V vs the SCE.The Xe-lamp (300 W) was kept at a distance of 10 cm from the working electrode.

catalyst.source: In this work, AurGOg-C 3 N 4 composites as novel 0D2D2D hybrid photocatalyst has successfully been fabricated via hydrothermal and photo-deposition methods.
catalyst.context: In this work, AurGOg-C 3 N 4 composites as novel 0D2D2D hybrid photocatalyst has successfully been fabricated via hydrothermal and photo-deposition methods.The amount optimized 2Au0.6rGOg-C 3 N 4 composite exhibits enhanced visible-light activity for H 2 evolutionCO 2 reduction with apparent quantum efficiency of 3.821.98 at 420 nm.We demonstrate by combined electrochemical CV, RRDE and UPS measurements that water oxidization in this hybrid system occurs via an unconventional two-electron two-step pathway through intermediate H 2 O 2 formation.This process is well known for fast reaction speed, leading to enhanced photocatalytic efficiency.The dual functions of rGO for both efficient charge transfer and decomposition of intermediate H 2 O 2 is shown to be critical to realize this performance enhancement.Overall, the merits of rGO, combined with SPR effect of Au, promote visible-light absorption, catalytic stability, electron mobility, and charge separation of the low-dimensional hybrid system, thus leading to enhanced water splitting efficiency in the visible-light range.This work will provide new directions for the rational design of visible-light active g-C 3 N 4 -based photocatalysts for solar fuel production. Here we are submitting the revised manuscript entitle of "Promoting Visible-Light Photocatalytic Activities for Carbon Nitride Based 0D2D2D Hybrid System: Beyond the Conventional 4-Electron Mechanism".There is no any conflict of co-authors.I am responsible for this paper. Our work provides feasible routes for the rational design of the visible-light responsive low-dimensional CN-based highly efficient hybrid photocatalysts for solar fuel production.Therefore, we believe our results are important and can be helpful for the relevant research community. The current energy crisis and environmental problem are big issue which we are facing.Thus solar energy harvesting and conversion into useful chemical energy with the aid of semiconductor photocatalysts is a promising technique to solve both energy and environmental issues.However, it is a great challenge to develop abundant and stable photocatalysts that is effective under visible-light.Although the star material carbon nitride (CN) has many merits, the efficiency of CN is still not satisfactory for practical applications owing to the fast recombination rate of photogenerated charges and limited visible-light absorption. Fig. 3.The elemental mappings images (A) of 2Au0.6rGOg-C3N4,(B) of Carbon, (C) of Nitrogen, (D) of Oxygen, and (E) of Au elements.(F) EDX spectrum of 2Au 0.6rGOg-C3N4 composite.The elemental mappings clearly show that all the elements are equally distributed. Fig. 4. XPS survey spectra (A) of g-C 3 N 4 , 0.6rGOg-C 3 N 4 and 2Au0.6rGOg-C 3 N 4 composites.The magnified XPS spectra (B) of N1 s, (C) of C1 s, and (D) of Au4f elements. Fig. 5. Photoluminescence spectra (A), further to confirm the charge separation mechanism here we also measured fluorescence spectra by using coumarin (B) inset single wavelength (520 nm) fluorescent spectra to investigate the plasmonic effect of Au (C) photocurrent density spectra (PEC performance was measured in 0.5 mol L -1 NaOH electrolyte solution.A three electrode cell was used with the testing film as the working electrode, an AgAgCl (saturated KCl solution) as a reference electrode, and Pt plate was used as counter electrode) and (D) electrochemical impedance spectra Nynquist plots of 0.6rGOg-C 3 N 4 , 0.5Au0.6rGOg-C3 N 4 , 1Au0.6rGOg-C 3 N 4 , 2Au0.6rGOg-C 3 N 4 and 4Au0.6rGOg-C 3 N 4 photocatalysts. photocorrosion even after 6 photocatalytic cycles (each cycle of 6 h), indicatingthat the fabricated composite is highly stable.Further to support the photocatalytic activities of AurGOg-C 3 N 4 , here we also evaluate the photocatalytic activities for CO 2 reduction.As shown in Fig.6C, D and SI-Fig.11,similar photoactivity results are demonstrated for g-C 3 N 4 , rGOg-C 3 N 4 and AurGOg-C 3 N 4 during the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 .It is confirmed that different gas-phase products such as CH 4 , CO, and very negligible amount of H 2 and O 2 are detected as a result of CO 2 photo-reduction in the presence of H 2 O as the reducing agent.It is worth noting that the CH 4 rate for 2AurGOg-C 3 N 4 sample is quilt linear with time with nearly 6 molgh.The aforementioned results demonstrate much higher visible-light photoactivity for CO 2 conversion as compared with pristine g-C 3 N 4 (3 mol).It should be noticed from Fig.6C,D, that the amounts of produced H 2 and CH 4 after 8 h of photocatalytic reactions still keep a constant increasing trend in the photocatalytic performance, indicating high stability of CO 2 reduction for the photocatalyst7374757677. light source.source: The optimized sample 2Au0.6rGOg-C 3 N 4 exhibits exceptional visible-light activity for water splititng and CO 2 reduction with quantum efficiency of 3.82 and 1.98 , respectively.
light source.context: Photocatalysis is regarded as one of promising technology for future clean and sustaniable energy applications.Herein, we have fabricated Au-modified reduced graphene oxide coupled with carbon nitride (AurGOg-C 3 N 4 ) as novel 0D2D2D photocatalytic nanocomposites.The optimized sample 2Au0.6rGOg-C 3 N 4 exhibits exceptional visible-light activity for water splititng and CO 2 reduction with quantum efficiency of 3.82 and 1.98 , respectively.Electrochemistry and ultraviolent photoemission are combined to determine the band alignments and elaborate associated water splitting path-way mechanism.It is validated that due to intrinsic deep valence band position of g-C 3 N 4 , the obtained nanocomposites exhibit unusual two-step two-electron way of water oxidization through intermediate H 2 O 2 catalyzed by rGO addition.The exceptional photoactivities are attributed to dual functions of enhanced charge separation and two-electron water oxidization facilitated by rGO and surface plasmon effect of decorated Au.Our work provides illuminations for low cost and high efficiency photocatalytic water splitting and CO 2 reduction applications.

light source.source: The reaction system was evacuated for 30 min and then irradiated under visible-light ( 420 nm) with a 300 W Xe-lamp.
light source.context: All the reagents were directly used as received without further purification. To prepare g-C 3 N 4 , approximately 10 g of urea was directly annealed in a muffle furnace at 550 C (0.5 C min -1 ) for 4 h, and then crushed into fine powder. To fabricate rGOg-C 3 N 4 nanocomposites, 2 g of the fabricated g-C 3 N 4 was dispersed into a mixture of de-ionized H 2 O and ethanol (1:4).The suspensions were kept under stirring for 2 h, and then 0.2, 0.4 and 0.6 g (10 wt , 20 wt and 30 wt ) of rGO was added to each suspension and vigorously stirred for 2 h.The suspensions were dried in at 85 C and then annealed at 450 C for 2 h to obtain different proportion rGOg-C 3 N 4 nanocomposites. Different wt.percentage (i.e.0.5 , 1.0 , 2.0 , and 4.0 ) of Au was photo-deposited on the photocatalysts surfaces.The required amount of Au solution (HAuCl 4 4H 2 O) was mixed with 50 mL de-ionized H 2 O.After that, 1 g of the rGOg-C 3 N 4 composites was dispersed in 5050 (100) mL watermethanol contained in a 250 mL beaker.Then, the appropriate amount of Au solution was added to each composite mixture and irradiated under visible-light irradiation for 2 h with continuous stirring.The samples were then centrifuged and dried at 85 C.The obtained slightly dark photocatalysts were labeled, according to the composition, as 0.5Au 0.6rGOg-C 3 N 4 , 1Au 0.6rGOg-C 3 N 4 , 2Au 0.6rGOg-C 3 N 4 and 4Au0.6rGOg-C 3 N 4 , respectively. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of the photocatalysts were obtained with Bruker D8 (Germany), using Cu K radiation source.The TEM images were captured with a JEOL-JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope.The SEM images were captured with Hitachi-S-4800 Scanning electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan) 67,68.The FT-IR spectra were taken with a Bruker Equinox-55 spectrophotometer.The XPS analysis was carried out with a Kratos-Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectroscope with Al (mono) X-ray source.The adventitious carbon (binding energy 284.6 eV) was used as a reference for samples calibration.The DRS spectra were obtained with UV-2550 (Shimadzu, Japan) UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectrophotometer.The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained at 420 nm excitation wavelength, using a PE-LS-55 spectro-fluoro-photometer 697071. The H 2 production experiments were performed in an online quartz cylindrical cell with area 6.5 cm 2 and volume 250 mL, linked to a closed gas circulatory system, where the evolved gases are detected with an online gas chromatograph TCD (Tech, GC-7900, N 2 gas carrier).For photocatalytic experiment, about 0.1 g of photocatalyst was dispersed in a mixture of 80 mL de-ionized H 2 O and 20 mL methanol (Note: in overall H 2 O splitting 100 mL pure H 2 O was used).The reaction system was evacuated for 30 min and then irradiated under visible-light ( 420 nm) with a 300 W Xe-lamp.The amount of H 2 gas was measured at regular time interval of 1 h. In the photocatalytic CO 2 reduction process, for each experiment, 0.1 g of a sample was dispersed in 50 mL water in a cylindrical steel reactor with 100 mL volume and glass surface area 6 cm 2 .A 300 W xenon arc lamp was used as the light source with a cut off filter of 420 nm.Highly pure CO 2 gas was entered into the reaction setup for reaching ambient pressure.The used photocatalyst was allowed to equilibrate in the CO 2 H 2 O system for 1 h.During irradiation, about 0.5 mL of gas products were continually taken from the reaction cell at a given time interval for subsequent CH 4 , CO, H 2 and O 2 concentration analysis by using a gas chromatograph (TCD and FID both). To measure the OH amount, about 0.05 g of photocatalyst was dispersed in a quartz reactor containing 40 mL of 0.001 M coumarin aqueous solution.The reactor was stirred for 30 min to attain the adsorption equilibrium.After that, the photocatalyst was irradiated under visible-light for 1 h and then centrifuged.A certain amount was taken into a Pyrex glass cell for the fluorescence measurement of 7-hydroxycoumarin at excitation wavelength of 420 nm, through a spectrofluorometer (Perkin-Elmer LS 55). To confirm the presence of H 2 O 2 as a reaction intermediate, 0.05 g sample was mixed with 40 mL deionized water by magnetic stirrer for 10 min in the absence of light, in order to keep the reactive system uniform adsorption equilibrium.After that the sample is exposed under light irradiation for 1 h and then centrifuged.The H 2 O 2 measurements test were performed in 2.0 mL reaction solution after the irradiation centrifuged with 0.5 mL of 1 o-tolidine in 0.1 M HCl.After 2 min, 1 M HCl (2 mL) was added to the above solution and the absorption spectrum of the solution was immediately recorded. For PEC studies, approximately 0.05 g of photocatalyst was dispersed in 2 mL isopropyl alcohol and stirred for 1 h.Then, 0.025 g of Macrogol-6000 was grinded into fine powder and added to the suspension.The suspension was kept in ultrasonic bath for 30 min, and then stirred for 1 h.After that, 0.05 mL of acetyl acetone was drop wise added to the suspension and stirred for 72 h.The cleaned conductivefluorine-doped tin-oxide (FTO) coated glasses were used as substrates for the preparation of photocatalysts films.The films were prepared by a doctor blade method-using Scotch tape as a spacer.The prepared photocatalysts films were dried in air for 30 min.The FTO glasses with films were cut into 1.0 cm 3.0 cm pieces.The films surface area was 1.0 cm 1.0 cm.For photoelectrode preparation, an electrical connection was made with FTO substrate using silver conducting paste linked to a copper-wire and enclosed in a glass tube.The working arithmetic surface area of the thin film was kept as 0.5 cm 0.5 cm and the extra area was covered with epoxy resin 53,54.The cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electron transfer number (n) experiments were performed in a three electrode configuration cell connected to a computer controlled CHI 920C work-station.The Pt and AgAgCl electrodes were used as the counter-electrode and the reference electrode, respectively.A glassy carbon (GC) electrode was clearly washed with Al 2 O 3 .After that, 7 L of the 0.5 wt Nafion solution was dropped onto the GC electrode surface.Then, 20 L of the catalyst solution having 1.5 mg mL -1 pasted on the surface of GC electrode and dried in oven at 40C.Before the test, the working electrode was immersed in the electrolyte solution and activated for 30 min to keep it stable.The CV curves were carried out in 0.1 M BMIMPF 6 solution (de-ionized water) in the presence of N 2 -environment with a 50 mVs scan rate.The Xelamp with 420 nm cut-off filter was used as the irradiation source.The electron-transfer number was recorded with a rotating disk-ring electrode (RRDE).The RRDE collection tests for 2Au0.6rGOCNwere carried out in N 2 -saturated ultrapure water a scan rate of 100 mVs and rotation speed of 1600 rpm.The disk potential was set at open circuit potential.The ring-potential was set at 0.9 V vs the SCE.The Xe-lamp (300 W) was kept at a distance of 10 cm from the working electrode.

reaction medium.context: In this work, AurGOg-C 3 N 4 composites as novel 0D2D2D hybrid photocatalyst has successfully been fabricated via hydrothermal and photo-deposition methods.The amount optimized 2Au0.6rGOg-C 3 N 4 composite exhibits enhanced visible-light activity for H 2 evolutionCO 2 reduction with apparent quantum efficiency of 3.821.98 at 420 nm.We demonstrate by combined electrochemical CV, RRDE and UPS measurements that water oxidization in this hybrid system occurs via an unconventional two-electron two-step pathway through intermediate H 2 O 2 formation.This process is well known for fast reaction speed, leading to enhanced photocatalytic efficiency.The dual functions of rGO for both efficient charge transfer and decomposition of intermediate H 2 O 2 is shown to be critical to realize this performance enhancement.Overall, the merits of rGO, combined with SPR effect of Au, promote visible-light absorption, catalytic stability, electron mobility, and charge separation of the low-dimensional hybrid system, thus leading to enhanced water splitting efficiency in the visible-light range.This work will provide new directions for the rational design of visible-light active g-C 3 N 4 -based photocatalysts for solar fuel production. Here we are submitting the revised manuscript entitle of "Promoting Visible-Light Photocatalytic Activities for Carbon Nitride Based 0D2D2D Hybrid System: Beyond the Conventional 4-Electron Mechanism".There is no any conflict of co-authors.I am responsible for this paper. Our work provides feasible routes for the rational design of the visible-light responsive low-dimensional CN-based highly efficient hybrid photocatalysts for solar fuel production.Therefore, we believe our results are important and can be helpful for the relevant research community. The current energy crisis and environmental problem are big issue which we are facing.Thus solar energy harvesting and conversion into useful chemical energy with the aid of semiconductor photocatalysts is a promising technique to solve both energy and environmental issues.However, it is a great challenge to develop abundant and stable photocatalysts that is effective under visible-light.Although the star material carbon nitride (CN) has many merits, the efficiency of CN is still not satisfactory for practical applications owing to the fast recombination rate of photogenerated charges and limited visible-light absorption. reactor type.context: All the reagents were directly used as received without further purification. To prepare g-C 3 N 4 , approximately 10 g of urea was directly annealed in a muffle furnace at 550 C (0.5 C min -1 ) for 4 h, and then crushed into fine powder. To fabricate rGOg-C 3 N 4 nanocomposites, 2 g of the fabricated g-C 3 N 4 was dispersed into a mixture of de-ionized H 2 O and ethanol (1:4).The suspensions were kept under stirring for 2 h, and then 0.2, 0.4 and 0.6 g (10 wt , 20 wt and 30 wt ) of rGO was added to each suspension and vigorously stirred for 2 h.The suspensions were dried in at 85 C and then annealed at 450 C for 2 h to obtain different proportion rGOg-C 3 N 4 nanocomposites. Different wt.percentage (i.e.0.5 , 1.0 , 2.0 , and 4.0 ) of Au was photo-deposited on the photocatalysts surfaces.The required amount of Au solution (HAuCl 4 4H 2 O) was mixed with 50 mL de-ionized H 2 O.After that, 1 g of the rGOg-C 3 N 4 composites was dispersed in 5050 (100) mL watermethanol contained in a 250 mL beaker.Then, the appropriate amount of Au solution was added to each composite mixture and irradiated under visible-light irradiation for 2 h with continuous stirring.The samples were then centrifuged and dried at 85 C.The obtained slightly dark photocatalysts were labeled, according to the composition, as 0.5Au 0.6rGOg-C 3 N 4 , 1Au 0.6rGOg-C 3 N 4 , 2Au 0.6rGOg-C 3 N 4 and 4Au0.6rGOg-C 3 N 4 , respectively. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of the photocatalysts were obtained with Bruker D8 (Germany), using Cu K radiation source.The TEM images were captured with a JEOL-JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope.The SEM images were captured with Hitachi-S-4800 Scanning electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan) 67,68.The FT-IR spectra were taken with a Bruker Equinox-55 spectrophotometer.The XPS analysis was carried out with a Kratos-Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectroscope with Al (mono) X-ray source.The adventitious carbon (binding energy 284.6 eV) was used as a reference for samples calibration.The DRS spectra were obtained with UV-2550 (Shimadzu, Japan) UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectrophotometer.The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained at 420 nm excitation wavelength, using a PE-LS-55 spectro-fluoro-photometer 697071. The H 2 production experiments were performed in an online quartz cylindrical cell with area 6.5 cm 2 and volume 250 mL, linked to a closed gas circulatory system, where the evolved gases are detected with an online gas chromatograph TCD (Tech, GC-7900, N 2 gas carrier).For photocatalytic experiment, about 0.1 g of photocatalyst was dispersed in a mixture of 80 mL de-ionized H 2 O and 20 mL methanol (Note: in overall H 2 O splitting 100 mL pure H 2 O was used).The reaction system was evacuated for 30 min and then irradiated under visible-light ( 420 nm) with a 300 W Xe-lamp.The amount of H 2 gas was measured at regular time interval of 1 h. In the photocatalytic CO 2 reduction process, for each experiment, 0.1 g of a sample was dispersed in 50 mL water in a cylindrical steel reactor with 100 mL volume and glass surface area 6 cm 2 .A 300 W xenon arc lamp was used as the light source with a cut off filter of 420 nm.Highly pure CO 2 gas was entered into the reaction setup for reaching ambient pressure.The used photocatalyst was allowed to equilibrate in the CO 2 H 2 O system for 1 h.During irradiation, about 0.5 mL of gas products were continually taken from the reaction cell at a given time interval for subsequent CH 4 , CO, H 2 and O 2 concentration analysis by using a gas chromatograph (TCD and FID both). To measure the OH amount, about 0.05 g of photocatalyst was dispersed in a quartz reactor containing 40 mL of 0.001 M coumarin aqueous solution.The reactor was stirred for 30 min to attain the adsorption equilibrium.After that, the photocatalyst was irradiated under visible-light for 1 h and then centrifuged.A certain amount was taken into a Pyrex glass cell for the fluorescence measurement of 7-hydroxycoumarin at excitation wavelength of 420 nm, through a spectrofluorometer (Perkin-Elmer LS 55). To confirm the presence of H 2 O 2 as a reaction intermediate, 0.05 g sample was mixed with 40 mL deionized water by magnetic stirrer for 10 min in the absence of light, in order to keep the reactive system uniform adsorption equilibrium.After that the sample is exposed under light irradiation for 1 h and then centrifuged.The H 2 O 2 measurements test were performed in 2.0 mL reaction solution after the irradiation centrifuged with 0.5 mL of 1 o-tolidine in 0.1 M HCl.After 2 min, 1 M HCl (2 mL) was added to the above solution and the absorption spectrum of the solution was immediately recorded.
